prompt: According to 3 John 1, how does the writer describe himself at the beginning of the letter?
choices_json: The elder
The prisoner
The apostle to the nations
The servant in Patmos
correct_answer: The elder
explanation: The letter opens with the words, 'The elder to Gaius the beloved' (3 John 1:1).

prompt: According to 3 John 1, to whom is the letter addressed?
choices_json: Gaius
Demetrius
Diotrephes
Caiaphas
correct_answer: Gaius
explanation: The elder addresses the letter 'to Gaius the beloved' (3 John 1:1).

prompt: According to 3 John 1, what does the elder pray will prosper along with Gaius's health?
choices_json: His soul
His business
His household
His reputation in Rome
correct_answer: His soul
explanation: The elder prays that Gaius may prosper and be in health, 'even as your soul prospers' (3 John 1:2).

prompt: According to 3 John 1, what brought the elder great joy regarding Gaius?
choices_json: Brothers testified that he walked in truth
He had traveled safely to Ephesus
He had rebuilt the meeting house
He had become a famous teacher
correct_answer: Brothers testified that he walked in truth
explanation: The elder rejoiced greatly when brothers came and testified about Gaius's truth, how he walked in truth (3 John 1:3).

prompt: According to 3 John 1, what does the elder say he has no greater joy than?
choices_json: Hearing that his children walk in truth
Seeing churches grow in number
Receiving gifts from believers
Traveling with faithful brothers
correct_answer: Hearing that his children walk in truth
explanation: He writes, 'I have no greater joy than this, to hear about my children walking in truth' (3 John 1:4).

prompt: According to 3 John 1, what kind of work was Gaius doing for the brothers and strangers?
choices_json: A faithful work
A secret work
A burdensome work
An unfinished work
correct_answer: A faithful work
explanation: The elder says Gaius does 'a faithful work in whatever you accomplish for those who are brothers and strangers' (3 John 1:5).

prompt: According to 3 John 1, for whose sake had certain brothers gone out?
choices_json: For the sake of the Name
For the sake of Moses
For the sake of Caesar
For the sake of the temple
correct_answer: For the sake of the Name
explanation: The letter says they went out 'for the sake of the Name' (3 John 1:7).

prompt: According to 3 John 1, from whom were those traveling brothers taking nothing?
choices_json: The Gentiles
The Samaritans
The priests
The tax collectors
correct_answer: The Gentiles
explanation: The elder says they took nothing from the Gentiles (3 John 1:7).

prompt: According to 3 John 1, whom does the elder say loves to be first among them?
choices_json: Diotrephes
Demetrius
Gaius
Silvanus
correct_answer: Diotrephes
explanation: The elder writes, 'Diotrephes, who loves to be first among them' (3 John 1:9).

prompt: According to 3 John 1, who has the testimony of all and of the truth itself?
choices_json: Demetrius
Diotrephes
Gaius
The elder
correct_answer: Demetrius
explanation: The elder says, 'Demetrius has the testimony of all, and of the truth itself' (3 John 1:12).

prompt: According to 3 John 1, why did the elder rejoice greatly?
choices_json: Because brothers came and testified to Gaius's truth
Because Diotrephes had repented publicly
Because Demetrius had built a new church
Because the Gentiles had begun to support the mission
correct_answer: Because brothers came and testified to Gaius's truth
explanation: He rejoiced greatly when brothers came and bore witness to Gaius's truth and his walking in truth (3 John 1:3).

prompt: According to 3 John 1, how had Gaius shown love before the assembly?
choices_json: By helping the brothers and strangers on their journey
By debating false teachers in public
By sending money to Jerusalem only
By refusing to host any travelers
correct_answer: By helping the brothers and strangers on their journey
explanation: The brothers testified before the assembly to Gaius's love, and the elder says he will do well to send them forward worthily of God (3 John 1:5-6).

prompt: According to 3 John 1, how should Gaius send the traveling brothers on their journey?
choices_json: In a manner worthy of God
As quickly as possible
With letters from Rome
In secret to avoid attention
correct_answer: In a manner worthy of God
explanation: The elder says, 'You will do well to send them forward on their journey in a manner worthy of God' (3 John 1:6).

prompt: According to 3 John 1, why ought believers receive such workers?
choices_json: So they may become fellow workers for the truth
So they may gain status in the churches
So they may avoid persecution from Rome
So they may replace local elders quickly
correct_answer: So they may become fellow workers for the truth
explanation: The elder says believers ought to receive such people 'that we may be fellow workers for the truth' (3 John 1:8).

prompt: According to 3 John 1, how had Diotrephes responded to the elder and his companions?
choices_json: He did not accept them
He welcomed them openly
He traveled with them briefly
He sent aid for their mission
correct_answer: He did not accept them
explanation: The elder says Diotrephes 'doesn't accept us' (3 John 1:9).

prompt: According to 3 John 1, what was Diotrephes doing with brothers who wanted to receive visiting believers?
choices_json: Forbidding them and throwing them out of the assembly
Training them for missionary travel
Sending them to Demetrius for counsel
Asking them to wait for the elder's letter
correct_answer: Forbidding them and throwing them out of the assembly
explanation: Diotrephes would not receive the brothers, forbade those who wanted to, and threw them out of the assembly (3 John 1:10).

prompt: According to 3 John 1, what instruction does the elder give Gaius about imitation?
choices_json: Do not imitate what is evil, but what is good
Imitate the strongest speaker in the church
Avoid imitating any teacher at all
Imitate only those from your own family
correct_answer: Do not imitate what is evil, but what is good
explanation: The elder writes, 'Don't imitate that which is evil, but that which is good' (3 John 1:11).

prompt: According to 3 John 1, what does the elder hope to do soon instead of writing more with ink and pen?
choices_json: See Gaius and speak face to face
Send another messenger from Jerusalem
Visit the Gentiles who supported the mission
Write a public letter to the whole province
correct_answer: See Gaius and speak face to face
explanation: The elder says he hopes to see Gaius soon and they will speak face to face (3 John 1:13-14).

prompt: According to 3 John 1, how is Demetrius further commended besides receiving testimony from all?
choices_json: He has the testimony of the truth itself
He was healed from a serious illness
He was appointed over many churches
He supported the brothers from Gentile gifts
correct_answer: He has the testimony of the truth itself
explanation: The elder says Demetrius has testimony from all, 'and of the truth itself' (3 John 1:12).

prompt: According to 3 John 1, what does the elder's prayer for Gaius's health imply about Gaius's spiritual condition?
choices_json: His soul was already prospering well
He had abandoned the truth recently
He was spiritually weak but physically strong
He was unknown to the churches
correct_answer: His soul was already prospering well
explanation: The elder prays Gaius may prosper in health 'even as your soul prospers,' implying Gaius's spiritual condition was strong (3 John 1:2).

prompt: According to 3 John 1, what does Gaius's hospitality reveal about his character?
choices_json: His love was active and faithful toward truth-serving workers
He preferred strangers over fellow believers
He was trying to gain power over the assembly
He wanted the Gentiles to finance the mission
correct_answer: His love was active and faithful toward truth-serving workers
explanation: Gaius is praised for faithful action toward brothers and strangers who testified of his love and were to be helped forward worthily of God (3 John 1:5-6).

prompt: According to 3 John 1, what does the phrase 'for the sake of the Name' suggest about the traveling workers?
choices_json: They were serving Christ's mission with devoted purpose
They were seeking a reputation for themselves
They were traveling to collect taxes for the churches
They were leaving because of family disputes only
correct_answer: They were serving Christ's mission with devoted purpose
explanation: The letter says they went out for the sake of the Name, indicating mission centered on Christ and his cause (3 John 1:7).

prompt: According to 3 John 1, what does the contrast between Gaius and Diotrephes mainly highlight?
choices_json: The difference between serving the truth and seeking prominence
The difference between wealth and poverty in the churches
The difference between Jews and Gentiles in the mission
The difference between older and younger believers
correct_answer: The difference between serving the truth and seeking prominence
explanation: Gaius is praised for faithful hospitality that advances the truth, while Diotrephes is criticized for loving to be first and rejecting the brothers (3 John 1:5-10).

prompt: According to 3 John 1, what does becoming 'fellow workers for the truth' imply about supporting missionaries?
choices_json: Those who support faithful workers share in the ministry of truth
Only ordained elders can take part in gospel work
Support matters less than public teaching ability
Financial help alone is the whole mission
correct_answer: Those who support faithful workers share in the ministry of truth
explanation: The elder says receiving such workers makes believers 'fellow workers for the truth,' showing partnership in gospel ministry (3 John 1:8).

prompt: According to 3 John 1, why is Diotrephes's behavior more serious than personal rudeness?
choices_json: It harms the church's welcome of faithful workers and resists apostolic authority
It only shows he preferred shorter letters
It means he misunderstood travel schedules briefly
It mainly concerns his dislike of Demetrius alone
correct_answer: It harms the church's welcome of faithful workers and resists apostolic authority
explanation: Diotrephes does not accept the elder, speaks wicked words, refuses the brothers, and expels those who would receive them, making his behavior damaging to the church (3 John 1:9-10).

prompt: According to 3 John 1, what is the force of the command not to imitate evil but good?
choices_json: Believers should discern examples carefully and choose conduct aligned with God
Believers should avoid learning from anyone around them
Believers should copy only famous leaders
Believers should ignore actions and focus only on words
correct_answer: Believers should discern examples carefully and choose conduct aligned with God
explanation: The elder tells Gaius not to imitate evil but good, then links doing good with being of God and doing evil with not seeing God (3 John 1:11).

prompt: According to 3 John 1, what does the commendation of Demetrius contribute to the letter?
choices_json: It provides a positive example of approved character after the warning about Diotrephes
It replaces Gaius as the main recipient of the letter
It shows every traveler should be received without discernment
It introduces a dispute over church finances
correct_answer: It provides a positive example of approved character after the warning about Diotrephes
explanation: After warning about Diotrephes, the elder commends Demetrius with strong testimony, offering a contrasting example of approved character (3 John 1:11-12).

prompt: How do 3 John 1:5-8 and 3 John 1:9-10 work together in the letter's argument?
choices_json: They contrast faithful support of gospel workers with selfish opposition to them
They show two equally valid ways of leading the church
They compare local ministry with temple worship
They explain why written letters are better than visits
correct_answer: They contrast faithful support of gospel workers with selfish opposition to them
explanation: Gaius is praised for helping the brothers and becoming a fellow worker for the truth, while Diotrephes rejects them and expels those who receive them (3 John 1:5-10).

prompt: How does the theme of truth shape the whole letter of 3 John 1?
choices_json: Truth is seen in Gaius's walk, the mission of the brothers, and the approval of Demetrius
Truth appears only in the opening greeting and nowhere else
Truth is mainly about legal disputes inside the church
Truth is treated as less important than peace at any cost
correct_answer: Truth is seen in Gaius's walk, the mission of the brothers, and the approval of Demetrius
explanation: The elder rejoices that Gaius walks in truth, says believers become fellow workers for the truth, and says Demetrius has testimony from the truth itself (3 John 1:3-4, 8, 12).

prompt: What role does hospitality play in the structure and purpose of 3 John 1?
choices_json: It serves as a concrete test of whether someone advances or hinders the truth
It is mentioned only as a minor personal preference
It replaces doctrinal faithfulness as the central issue
It is treated as unnecessary when leaders disagree
correct_answer: It serves as a concrete test of whether someone advances or hinders the truth
explanation: Gaius's hospitality supports truth-serving workers, whereas Diotrephes's refusal blocks them and harms the church, making hospitality a practical measure of faithfulness (3 John 1:5-10).

prompt: How does the elder's statement 'I have no greater joy than this' function in the letter?
choices_json: It frames the letter around spiritual faithfulness rather than mere personal affection
It shifts the focus away from truth to material success
It shows the elder values travel reports more than people
It introduces a list of church regulations only
correct_answer: It frames the letter around spiritual faithfulness rather than mere personal affection
explanation: The elder's greatest joy is hearing that his children walk in truth, setting spiritual faithfulness at the center of the letter's encouragement and rebuke (3 John 1:4).

prompt: Why is the elder's intention to confront Diotrephes significant in the flow of 3 John 1?
choices_json: It shows that abusive leadership in the church must be remembered and addressed
It means the elder had no evidence against him
It suggests Diotrephes had already been restored fully
It turns the letter into a civil lawsuit
correct_answer: It shows that abusive leadership in the church must be remembered and addressed
explanation: The elder says that if he comes, he will call attention to what Diotrephes is doing, showing that harmful leadership behavior requires direct accountability (3 John 1:10).

prompt: Why does 3 John 1 place the commendation of Demetrius after the warning about Diotrephes?
choices_json: To follow the negative example with a trustworthy positive model
To show Demetrius had replaced Diotrephes officially
To move the topic away from church life to personal travel
To prove that all conflict in the church had ended
correct_answer: To follow the negative example with a trustworthy positive model
explanation: After warning Gaius not to imitate evil, the letter commends Demetrius, giving a concrete example of one with good testimony (3 John 1:11-12).

prompt: How does the close of 3 John 1 affect the tone of the letter as a whole?
choices_json: It keeps the letter personal and pastoral despite its sharp warning
It turns the letter into a formal legal decree
It withdraws everything said earlier about truth
It shows the elder no longer planned to visit
correct_answer: It keeps the letter personal and pastoral despite its sharp warning
explanation: The elder closes with peace, greetings, and a hope to speak face to face, giving the brief letter a warm pastoral tone alongside its rebuke (3 John 1:13-15).

prompt: According to 3 John 1, what theological principle stands behind the call to support traveling brothers?
choices_json: Supporting faithful workers makes believers partners in the truth
Only wealthy Christians can honor God properly
Church authority depends mainly on hosting ability
Mission should rely on Gentile sponsorship whenever possible
correct_answer: Supporting faithful workers makes believers partners in the truth
explanation: The elder says believers ought to receive such workers so that 'we may be fellow workers for the truth' (3 John 1:8).

prompt: According to 3 John 1, what does the phrase 'hasn't seen God' mean in the contrast between good and evil conduct?
choices_json: Persistent evil conduct reveals a lack of real knowledge of God
Only visions can prove someone knows God
Seeing God refers mainly to physical eyesight
Anyone who sins once has permanently lost hope
correct_answer: Persistent evil conduct reveals a lack of real knowledge of God
explanation: The elder writes, 'He who does evil hasn't seen God,' linking conduct with true spiritual knowledge of God (3 John 1:11).

prompt: How does 3 John 1 connect personal piety and practical ministry?
choices_json: A prospering soul is expressed in truthful living and generous support for gospel work
Private spirituality is enough even without action
Good health matters more than faithful conduct
Mission work should be separated from character
correct_answer: A prospering soul is expressed in truthful living and generous support for gospel work
explanation: Gaius is described as one whose soul prospers, who walks in truth, and who faithfully supports traveling brothers, connecting inner spiritual health with outward ministry (3 John 1:2-6).

prompt: What makes Diotrephes's sin in 3 John 1 especially dangerous for the church?
choices_json: His desire for preeminence turns leadership into opposition against truth and faithful workers
He simply preferred different travel arrangements
He struggled only with private doubt and said nothing publicly
He objected to receiving Gentile believers into baptism
correct_answer: His desire for preeminence turns leadership into opposition against truth and faithful workers
explanation: Diotrephes loves to be first, rejects the elder, speaks maliciously, refuses the brothers, and expels those who receive them, showing how pride distorts leadership (3 John 1:9-10).

prompt: Why is Demetrius's testimony from 'the truth itself' especially weighty in 3 John 1?
choices_json: It means his character is consistent with the reality of the gospel, not just human praise
It means truth was a person traveling with him
It shows he had written more letters than the elder
It proves he was the leader of every church in the region
correct_answer: It means his character is consistent with the reality of the gospel, not just human praise
explanation: The elder says Demetrius has testimony from all and from the truth itself, suggesting his life aligns with the truth he professes (3 John 1:12).

prompt: How does 3 John 1 present a careful balance between love and discernment?
choices_json: It praises generous support for faithful workers while exposing a harmful leader by name
It says love requires ignoring every doctrinal or moral problem
It teaches discernment matters only for elders and not for friends
It avoids evaluating anyone in order to keep peace
correct_answer: It praises generous support for faithful workers while exposing a harmful leader by name
explanation: The letter warmly commends Gaius's love and hospitality toward faithful workers, yet also clearly warns about Diotrephes's destructive behavior (3 John 1:5-10).

prompt: What does 3 John 1 suggest about the relationship between truth and church order?
choices_json: Church life should be arranged to welcome and assist those serving the truth, not block them
Truth is unrelated to how leaders treat other believers
Church order exists mainly to protect powerful personalities
Hospitality and authority should never overlap
correct_answer: Church life should be arranged to welcome and assist those serving the truth, not block them
explanation: The elder commends receiving workers for the truth and condemns Diotrephes for blocking them and expelling supporters, linking truth to proper church conduct and order (3 John 1:8-10).
